rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim ('first end-to-end model...') is hedged with 'to the best of our knowledge' and placed at the very end rather than foregrounded; the gap statement is restated three times without sharpening. Making the piece 'stronger' plausibly requires tightening the thesis arc.
rationale.R1.2: The logical progression is inverted: results and architecture appear before motivation, and the motivation itself is duplicated in near-identical paragraphs (paragraphs 3 and 5–6). The argument cannot be 'stronger' without resolving these coherence failures.
rationale.R1.5: The artifact's section ordering is motivation→results→architecture→training→motivation-again, which is structurally broken. Two largely redundant motivation paragraphs follow the technical description. Structural reorganization is almost certainly load-bearing regardless of what 'stronger' means to the delegator.
rationale.R1.6: The most common reading of 'make this stronger' in academic prose revision is sentence-level sharpening — cutting hedges, tightening claims, improving rhetorical force. This is independently load-bearing from structural issues.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I focus primarily on sentence-level prose and rhetorical force (sharper claims, less hedging, tighter phrasing), or on structural fixes (reordering into motivation → method → results, eliminating the duplicated motivation paragraphs)?
